archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style

selected_question.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling through Instagram and you see two different ads for the same new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. Ad A opens with a slow-motion shot of lavender fields at golden hour, soft piano music, and a woman gently touching her glowing skin — no text on screen for the first 10 seconds. Ad B opens with a close-up of the ingredient label, a dermatologist explaining why each ingredient is gentle on sensitive skin, and a split-screen before/after of someone with skin redness. Which ad makes you stop scrolling, and what specifically about it grabs you in those first few seconds?
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly probes the first-few-seconds cognitive processing style — emotional/aesthetic vs. analytical/informational — in a context perfectly tailored to this person. Both ads feature elements she values (lavender, sensitive skin, natural ingredients, dermatologist credibility), so neither option is 'wrong' for her profile. The question forces her to reveal whether the emotional hook (mood, visuals, music) or the informational hook (ingredients, expert, evidence) is what actually captures her attention in the critical first moments. The 59 questions show she values both ingredient breakdowns AND has deep emotional attachment to scents/moods, creating genuine ambiguity that only this type of question can resolve.

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, Ad A would stop me mid-scroll. The lavender fields and that golden hour vibe — I'd feel it before I even know what the product is. I'm dramatic and moody like that; if something gives me a calming, feel-good visual, I'm already hooked. I'd probably watch the full thing and then go check the ingredients on the product page later on my own time.
archetypes.why_plausible: She describes herself as 'dramatic' and 'moody' (Q6), her fragrance attachment is deeply sensory and emotional ('flower scents = natural feel-good vibe,' Q37), and she values scent for 'internal well-being.' Her grooming is mood-dependent (Q32). These traits strongly support an emotional-first processing style where aesthetic resonance captures attention before analytical evaluation kicks in.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would rate a body wash concept significantly higher when it leads with sensory narrative language ('imagine lavender calming your sensitive skin after a long day') and evocative imagery. A feature-matrix concept with clinical data would feel cold and unappealing, scoring lower on emotional engagement and purchase intent despite containing the same information.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: This twin would watch a dreamy, music-driven Instagram video ad to completion and report high emotional resonance and purchase intent. An information-dense ad with ingredient callouts and dermatologist talking heads would feel clinical and boring — she'd skip within 3-5 seconds. Influencer content that 'sets a mood' would outperform tutorial-style ingredient breakdowns.

archetypes.answer_text: Ad B, no question. I have sensitive skin and I've been burned by products that look pretty but wreck my skin. The moment I see a dermatologist actually explaining why specific ingredients are safe, I'm paying attention. Pretty lavender fields tell me nothing about whether this will give me redness and bumps. I need to see what's in it before I feel anything about it.
archetypes.why_plausible: She ranks ingredients as her #1 purchase factor (Q11), prefers 'ingredient breakdown' content (Q57), rates packaging claims at 1/5 trust (Q17), and has a history of products that 'promised smooth skin but underdelivered' (Q53). Her sensitive skin concern (Q33) and experience with harsh products causing redness/bumps (Q30) create a rational, protective screening mechanism that would prioritize informational content over aesthetic appeal.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond most favorably to concepts structured around ingredient transparency, dermatologist endorsement, and specific sensitive-skin claims with evidence. A concept leading with poetic sensory language would be dismissed as 'marketing fluff' and score low on credibility, even if the underlying product is identical.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: This twin would skip aesthetic/mood-driven video ads within seconds, finding them uninformative. She'd engage deeply with ads featuring ingredient callouts, dermatologist explanations, and before/after evidence for sensitive skin. YouTube long-form ingredient review content would drive higher purchase intent than any Instagram Reel with beautiful visuals.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd probably pause at Ad A first — the lavender visuals and music would catch my eye because I'm a sucker for that aesthetic. But I wouldn't buy based on that alone. I'd then need to see the ingredient list and whether it's actually safe for sensitive skin. If Ad A doesn't give me that info within the next few seconds, I'd lose interest and maybe go find Ad B-type content on my own. Both matter, but the pretty one hooks me and the science one convinces me.
archetypes.why_plausible: This dual-processing style reconciles her seemingly contradictory traits: she's 'dramatic' and emotionally attached to scents/moods (Q6, Q37) but also deeply ingredient-focused (Q11), skeptical of packaging claims (Q17: 1/5), and researches extensively on YouTube/Amazon/Google before buying (Q58, Q59: never trusts friend recs without research). Her pattern is consistent with someone who gets emotionally drawn in but has a strong analytical verification gate before committing.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond best to concepts that layer emotional appeal with substantive proof — a sensory narrative opening ('lavender-mogra blend for skin that feels calm') followed immediately by ingredient credentials and sensitive-skin evidence. Concepts that are purely emotional or purely analytical would underperform compared to this layered approach, scoring moderate on both but high on neither.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: This twin would watch a visually beautiful ad longer than the pure analytical processor but would need informational payoff within the ad to convert. An ad that opens with mood/aesthetics and transitions to ingredient/dermatologist content in the second half would maximize both watch-through and purchase intent. A purely aesthetic ad would get views but low conversion; a purely informational ad would get skipped before the hook lands.

archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Aspirational Identity vs Pragmatic Self Orientation

selected_question.question_text: During Diwali, you receive a gifting budget of ₹1,500 from your office. You're considering a premium body wash gift set for yourself (₹899) that you'd never normally buy. Do you: A) Buy the premium set — it's essentially 'free money' B) Buy your regular body wash and use the rest of the budget on something else C) Gift the premium set to someone else and buy your regular one separately
selected_question.why_selected: This question perfectly isolates the aspirational vs pragmatic tension for this specific person. She normally spends ₹100-200 on body wash (Q21), is very loyal to her current brands (Q16), and has an ideal self that includes organic facemasks and holistic skin rituals (Q10). The 'free money' framing removes her usual price constraint — the key barrier — and forces a pure identity choice: does she seize the chance to experience the elevated self-care she fantasizes about (aspirational), or does she pragmatically redirect resources because premium body wash isn't something she actually values in practice? Her sensitive-skin concern (Q33), mood-dependent grooming (Q32), and the gap between her ₹250 ideal body wash (Q49) and her ₹100-200 actual spend (Q21) make all three responses plausible. No other question in the bank so cleanly separates identity aspiration from pragmatic adequacy while remaining personally relevant.

archetypes.answer_text: A) Buy the premium set for myself — honestly, I'd never spend ₹899 on body wash from my own pocket, but this feels like a guilt-free chance to finally try something luxurious. I always dream about upgrading my self-care routine with better organic products and weekend rituals, and this is the universe giving me permission. If it has lavender or something floral and is gentle on sensitive skin, I'd be so excited to use it.
archetypes.why_plausible: Her ideal routine includes organic facemasks and juice for skin health (Q10), she designed a ₹250 dream body wash with lavender-mogra blend (Q49), and she once spent ~₹350 on a body wash she was willing to experiment with (Q51). She clearly harbors aspirations beyond her current spend. The 'free money' framing removes her price sensitivity barrier, letting the aspirational self emerge.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would rate a premium Ayurvedic self-care body wash concept (₹400-500, spa-like positioning, artisanal ingredients) highly on appeal and purchase intent. She'd engage deeply with claims like 'elevate your daily ritual' and 'handcrafted with rare botanicals.' Willingness to pay would stretch to ₹350-450 if the concept aligns with her lavender/floral/sensitive-skin ideal.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: This twin would respond positively to aspirational lifestyle ads — a serene bathroom, soft lighting, a woman savoring a self-care moment with premium Ayurvedic body wash. Messaging like 'you deserve this ritual' or 'transform your everyday into extraordinary' would resonate. She'd save the ad, share it, and feel emotionally drawn to the brand identity.

archetypes.answer_text: B) I'd buy my regular body wash and use the rest on something I actually need — maybe a good face moisturizer or hand cream. ₹899 for a body wash gift set sounds fancy but I know I'll just go back to my Lux or Baylis & Harding anyway. My skin is sensitive and I'm not risking a flare-up for something pretty. I'd rather spend smart.
archetypes.why_plausible: She's extremely loyal to her current brands (Q16), repurchases the same product when it runs out (Q19), rates herself 1/7 on trying new brands (Q7), and her primary concern is sensitive skin safety (Q33). She's been burned before — the ₹350 milk-based product underdelivered (Q51-53). Her actual routine is minimal and functional (Q28-29), and grooming is mood-dependent (Q32), suggesting the daily reality is pragmatic, not ritualistic.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would dismiss premium self-care concepts as overpriced and irrelevant. A ₹450 'artisanal Ayurvedic spa ritual' body wash would score low on purchase intent (2-3/10). She'd focus on whether it's safe for sensitive skin and whether the price is justified by ingredients, not by experience or identity. She'd gravitate toward concepts positioned as 'gentle, effective, no-nonsense.'
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: This twin would find aspirational lifestyle ads disconnected and slightly annoying — 'that's not my real life.' She'd respond much better to a straightforward problem-solution ad: a woman with sensitive skin showing before/after, an ingredient breakdown, or a dermatologist explaining why the formulation is gentle. A normal bathroom setting and honest tone would build trust.

archetypes.answer_text: Hmm, it depends on what's in the set. If it's a brand I've heard of and the products are actually good for sensitive skin — like a nice lavender or floral body wash with natural ingredients — then A, I'd keep it for myself. But if it's some random luxury brand with harsh chemicals and strong synthetic fragrance, I'd gift it to someone and buy my regular stuff. I'm picky, so the premium tag alone doesn't impress me.
archetypes.why_plausible: This perfectly mirrors her personality — she describes herself as 'picky' (Q6), values ingredients above all else (Q11), and is a strong believer in natural/organic (Q12). She aspires to better products (Q10, Q45 — wants better sensitive-skin options) but filters everything through her sensitive-skin pragmatism. She prefers international brands specifically because they're gentler (Q14), not because they're premium per se. The conditional response reflects her real decision-making pattern.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would evaluate concepts on a dual filter: 'Does this feel elevated AND is it safe for my skin?' A premium Ayurvedic body wash with lavender, clear natural ingredients, and sensitive-skin claims would score high (7-8/10). The same premium concept without sensitive-skin reassurance or with vague 'luxury' positioning would score moderate (4-5/10). She'd be the hardest to predict without knowing concept specifics.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: This twin would engage with ads that blend aspiration with substance — a beautiful aesthetic paired with an ingredient breakdown or dermatologist endorsement. Pure lifestyle ads without product proof would feel shallow. Pure clinical ads without any emotional warmth would feel boring. The sweet spot is an ad that says 'here's something beautiful AND here's exactly why it works for your skin' — think a visually stunning Instagram reel that zooms into the ingredient list.

archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal

selected_question.question_text:
Imagine you're scrolling through Nykaa and see three body washes, all priced at ₹299, all claiming to be gentle on sensitive skin with natural ingredients. The only difference is their packaging design:

Bottle A: A white bottle with clean sans-serif text, a subtle blue medical cross icon, and a simple ingredient list on the front — it looks like something from a pharmacy.

Bottle B: A pastel lavender bottle with soft watercolor botanical illustrations, cursive font for the brand name, and a small 'handcrafted' badge — it looks like an artisanal gift-shop product.

Bottle C: A matte cream bottle with earthy green accents, a minimalist leaf line-drawing, modern sans-serif font, and lots of white space — it looks like a premium clean-beauty brand.

Which bottle do you click on first, and what about its design makes you trust it more for your sensitive skin? Would any of the other designs actively put you off, and why?
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly probes the unmeasured tension in her profile: she said 'clinical-looking' packaging (Q42) but also wants 'simple/cute' (Q49), prefers Ayurvedic/natural (Q12, Q18) yet gravitates toward international brands like Baylis & Harding (Q14). By presenting three distinct visual languages — pharmaceutical-clinical, artisanal-soft, and modern-minimalist — all at the same price and with the same claims, it isolates her visual semiotics processing. It forces her to reveal whether 'clinical' means pharmacy-white or clean-modern, whether botanical illustrations read as trustworthy-natural or frivolous, and how she resolves the clinical-vs-cute tension. This is highly predictive for both concept mockup reactions and ad creative direction.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: Clinical-literal: reads medical/pharmaceutical visual cues as most credible for sensitive skin
archetypes.answer_text: I'd click Bottle A first, honestly. When it comes to my sensitive skin, I need something that looks like it means business — that pharmacy-style design tells me they've actually thought about the formulation, not just making it look pretty. The watercolor one (B) is cute but gives me 'gift set you get at a wedding' vibes — I wouldn't trust it for my skin issues. The cream one (C) is fine but feels like it's trying too hard to be aesthetic. I want my body wash to look like it belongs next to a dermatologist's recommendation, not on an Instagram flat-lay.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with her Q42 preference for clinical-looking packaging, her trust in dermatologists (4/5 in Q17), her sensitive skin concerns (Q33), and her practical approach to skincare. Her preference for Baylis & Harding (Q14) — which has relatively clean, structured packaging — supports this. Her skepticism of marketing claims (packaging claims rated 1/5 in Q17) aligns with preferring no-nonsense design.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate a body wash concept with white/blue pharmaceutical packaging, clinical typography, and 'dermatologist-tested' badges highest. Would be skeptical of concepts using soft pastels, botanical illustrations, or handwritten fonts — perceiving them as prioritizing aesthetics over efficacy. Muted earth-tone Ayurvedic concepts would feel unscientific despite her stated Ayurvedic preference.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives using cool blues, clean white backgrounds, close-up ingredient shots, and clinical language. Warm golden tones and handwritten fonts in ads would feel inauthentic for sensitive-skin claims. Influencer content in lab-coat or bathroom-mirror settings would outperform lifestyle/garden settings. Would scroll past ads that look too 'pretty' or artisanal.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: Artisanal-soft: reads gentle, botanical visual cues as authentic signals of natural/gentle formulation
archetypes.answer_text: Bottle B would catch my eye first — the lavender colour and watercolor flowers just feel right for something that's supposed to be gentle and natural. It gives me that organic, handcrafted vibe which I associate with actually caring about what goes into the product. The white pharmacy bottle (A) looks too sterile and chemical-y to me, like it's hiding harsh ingredients behind a serious look. I like things that feel natural and have a soothing visual energy — if it looks like nature, it probably IS more natural. The cream one (C) is okay but a bit boring, doesn't make me feel anything.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with her strong 5/5 belief in natural/organic products (Q12), her love of lavender and mogra florals (Q36-37), her description of flower scents giving a 'natural feel-good vibe' (Q37), and wanting 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49). Her personality as 'dramatic' and 'moody' (Q6) suggests she responds emotionally to visual cues. Her ideal body wash is a lavender-mogra blend (Q49), so botanical illustrations would feel aligned with her product expectations.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate body wash concepts with floral watercolor illustrations, pastel palettes, and artisanal design language highest — perceiving them as genuinely natural and gentle. Would be less drawn to stark clinical concepts despite saying 'clinical-looking' in Q42. Ayurvedic concepts with traditional botanical motifs would score high. Would distrust bold, geometric modern designs as 'too corporate.'
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives featuring warm tones, botanical imagery, garden/nature settings, and soft cursive typography. Ads showing flowers, ingredients in their raw form (lavender sprigs, mogra blossoms), and handmade textures would build trust. Cool clinical ad aesthetics would feel cold and chemical. Would respond well to influencer content in natural-light, home-garden settings over sterile bathroom setups.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: Premium-minimalist: reads white space, modern typography, and restrained design as signals of quality and sophistication
archetypes.answer_text: Bottle C, definitely. It looks premium and clean without being over-the-top — the matte finish and white space give me confidence that this brand knows what it's doing. It's the kind of packaging I see on international brands like Baylis & Harding, which I already trust for my sensitive skin. Bottle A looks too much like a medicine — I want gentle, not hospital. And Bottle B is too busy and cutesy for me; it looks like something you'd find at a college fest stall. I like packaging that's simple but elevated — it tells me the brand spent money on the product, not just the decoration.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with her preference for international brands like Baylis & Harding (Q14) which use modern-minimalist design, her desire for 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49) where 'simple' maps to minimalism, and her clinical packaging preference (Q42) interpreted as 'clean and modern' rather than literally pharmaceutical. Her picky personality (Q6) and brand loyalty (Q16) suggest she gravitates toward polished, premium-coded design. Her willingness to pay up to ₹250 (Q49) and having paid ₹350 before (Q51) shows she reads premium cues.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate body wash concepts with matte finishes, restrained color palettes, modern sans-serif fonts, and generous white space highest. Would perceive these as both gentle (not aggressive) and credible (not frivolous). Would reject overly decorated or busy packaging as cheap, and overly clinical packaging as medicinal. Earth-tone Ayurvedic concepts would need modern typography to pass her quality filter.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ad creatives that use clean compositions, neutral color palettes with one accent color, modern typography, and aspirational lifestyle imagery. Would respond to 'elevated everyday' positioning — think Glossier or Aesop-style visual language adapted for Indian context. Ads that are too loud, too traditional, or too clinical would underperform. Influencer content in aesthetically curated spaces (minimalist bathrooms, well-lit vanities) would outperform both clinical and rustic settings.

archetypes_per_dim.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Marketing Claims and Framing

selected_question.question_text: What if your regular ₹299 body wash launched a 'dermatologist-recommended' version at ₹399 with the same ingredients but clinical testing certification? Would you upgrade? A) Yes — clinical validation is worth ₹100 more B) No — same ingredients means same product, it's just marketing C) I'd need to see the clinical study results first
selected_question.why_selected: This question is a near-perfect probe for this person's skepticism because it pits two things she already values against each other in a way that forces her to reveal how she processes marketing framing. She trusts dermatologists (4/5 in Q17), she cares deeply about sensitive skin (Q33), and she prioritizes ingredients (Q11, Q24). But she also rates packaging claims 1/5 (Q17), was burned by a premium product that 'underdelivered' (Q52-53), and is very loyal/repurchases the same product (Q16, Q19). This question asks: when the ingredients are identical but the marketing wrapper changes (adding 'dermatologist-recommended' + clinical certification), does she see through the framing as marketing or does her trust in dermatologists and sensitive-skin anxiety override her skepticism? Her answer directly reveals whether she applies skepticism selectively (trusting Ayurvedic/dermatologist claims but not general marketing) or universally. This has immediate implications for how she'd respond to body wash concepts that layer clinical evidence onto natural/Ayurvedic positioning.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: low-to-moderate skepticism (trusts claims aligned with her values)
archetypes.answer_text: I'd probably upgrade to the dermatologist-recommended version. If it's specifically tested for sensitive skin and a dermatologist has actually validated it, that ₹100 feels worth it for the peace of mind. I've had bad reactions before, so knowing there's clinical backing makes me more comfortable — especially if it's my regular brand that I already trust.
archetypes.why_plausible: She rates dermatologists 4/5 trust (Q17), has genuine sensitive skin concerns (Q33), and her ideal product is sensitive-skin safe (Q49). She's willing to pay 20-40% premium for Ayurvedic (Q15), and she already spends up to ₹350 on body wash (Q51). Her loyalty to trusted brands (Q16) means she'd view an upgrade from her existing brand as a natural extension rather than a marketing ploy.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would respond very positively to a concept claiming 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula for sensitive skin — 95% saw reduced irritation.' The combination of clinical authority and Ayurvedic positioning would feel credible and complementary. Purchase intent would be high (8-9/10) because both claim types reinforce her existing belief system. She'd accept '72-hour freshness with Ayurvedic botanicals' largely at face value.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: An influencer ad featuring a skincare expert explaining ingredients would feel authentic and informative. Before/after visuals (which she already prefers per Q57) would be taken as genuine evidence. A dermatologist endorsement in a YouTube-style ad would be highly persuasive. She'd engage positively with ads that layer scientific credibility onto natural/Ayurvedic messaging without questioning the combination.

archetypes.answer_text: No way — same ingredients means same product. This is classic marketing: slap a 'dermatologist-recommended' sticker on it and charge ₹100 more. I've already been burned paying ₹350 for something that was 'just okay.' If the formulation hasn't changed, the certification is just a label to justify a price hike. I'd rather stick with what I know works for my skin.
archetypes.why_plausible: She rates packaging claims 1/5 trust (Q17), was disappointed by a premium purchase that 'underdelivered' (Q52-53), found a social media purchase 'mediocre' (Q56), and ranks ingredients as her #1 purchase factor (Q11). Her self-described personality is 'sarcastic' and 'picky' (Q6). She repurchases the same product (Q19) and scores 1/10 on trying new brands (Q7), suggesting she's built defenses against marketing persuasion through past negative experiences.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would actively counter-argue against a concept claiming '72-hour freshness with Ayurvedic botanicals,' thinking 'that's a chemical performance claim dressed up in natural language — pick one.' A 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula' would feel contradictory and manipulative. Purchase intent would drop to 3-4/10 unless the ingredient list itself was compelling. She'd focus entirely on the ingredient panel and dismiss the positioning language.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Influencer endorsements would immediately trigger 'paid promotion' suspicion, even from skincare experts. Before/after visuals would be scrutinized as potentially manipulated. She'd skip past emotional or lifestyle-oriented ads entirely. Only a raw, no-frills ingredient breakdown video (like a YouTube deep-dive with no brand sponsorship) would earn her attention. Enthusiastic ad tones would actively repel her.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: moderate skepticism (skeptical but persuadable with evidence)
archetypes.answer_text: I'd need to see the actual clinical study results first. Like, what exactly did they test? How many people with sensitive skin were in the study? I don't trust a 'dermatologist-recommended' label on its own — that could mean one doctor glanced at it. But if there's a proper study showing it's genuinely better for sensitive skin, I'd consider paying the extra ₹100. I just won't take their word for it.
archetypes.why_plausible: She researches extensively on YouTube, Amazon, and Google before buying (Q58), scores 1/10 on trying things based on friend recommendations without her own research (Q59), and trusts dermatologists at 4/5 but packaging claims at 1/5 (Q17). This suggests she doesn't reject authority claims outright but demands verification. Her preference for 'Ingredient Breakdown' content (Q57) and checking reviews across multiple platforms (Q58) shows a verification-oriented mindset rather than blanket trust or blanket rejection.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would find a concept claiming 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula — 95% saw reduced irritation' initially interesting but would immediately want to know the study details. Purchase intent would sit at 5-6/10 at concept stage, rising to 8/10 if substantiation is provided or dropping to 3/10 if the brand can't back it up. She'd respond well to concepts that proactively include evidence (sample sizes, methodology) rather than just claims.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Before/after content would engage her but she'd look for signs of authenticity (real users vs. models, specific timelines, unfiltered skin). An influencer ad would get moderate engagement only if the influencer shows the ingredient list and explains the science — pure enthusiasm without substance would lose her. She'd be most persuaded by ads that anticipate skepticism and address it head-on, like 'here's the actual study' or 'we tested this on 500 women with sensitive skin — here's what happened.'

selected_question.question_text: Think about the last few times you came across a body wash ad or a new product recommendation — on Instagram, YouTube, or even in a store. Were there times you felt genuinely curious and open to it, and other times you just scrolled past or ignored it? What was different about YOU in those moments — your mood, your energy, whether you were stressed or relaxed, alone or with someone, browsing for fun or shopping with a purpose? Describe how your internal state at that moment affects whether you actually pay attention to or consider a new body wash.
selected_question.why_selected: None of the 291 bank questions address mood-state or situational context at the moment of marketing exposure — they all focus on price, deals, and purchase mechanics. This person explicitly identifies as 'moody' (Q6) and 'mood-dependent' about grooming (Q32), so we need a direct, open-ended probe that asks her to introspect on HOW her moods modulate her receptivity to product marketing. The open format matches her tendency to give detailed, personal, sometimes dramatic answers. It directly elicits the mechanism (comfort-seeking vs. defensive withdrawal vs. mood-irrelevant) that the dimension description identifies as unmeasured.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: High mood-congruent receptivity — bad moods increase openness to indulgent/comforting product messaging
archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, when I'm stressed or having a bad day, that's when I actually stop and look at those body wash ads — especially the ones showing someone just unwinding in a nice shower with lavender or florals. It feels like self-care I deserve. On a normal chill day I just scroll past because I already have my stuff sorted. My worst days are when I'm most likely to add something to cart, like treating myself to feel better.
archetypes.why_plausible: She describes grooming as 'mood-dependent' (Q32), values scent for 'internal well-being' (Q37), and her ideal routine includes organic facemasks as a weekend ritual (Q10) — all suggesting she already frames personal care as emotional self-care. Her self-described dramatic and moody personality (Q6) is consistent with someone who uses products as mood repair tools. Her preference for lavender/floral scents tied to 'feel-good vibes' (Q37) aligns with comfort-seeking behavior.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: When shown a body wash concept positioned around relaxation, stress-relief, or 'end-of-day unwinding' with lavender/mogra notes, this twin would rate it very highly — especially if the concept evokes emotional comfort. Functional or clinical concepts shown during a neutral mood would get lukewarm scores. Concepts with indulgent sensory language would consistently outperform rational claims for this twin.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: A slow-paced, atmospheric ad showing a woman de-stressing in a warm shower with soft lighting and floral scent cues would deeply resonate — she'd say 'this is exactly what I need.' Upbeat, energetic ads or humor-driven campaigns would fall flat or feel irrelevant. Influencer content showing 'bad day self-care routines' would drive the highest engagement. She'd be most persuadable through evening/nighttime ad placements.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: High mood-incongruent resistance — bad moods trigger skepticism and rejection of all marketing
archetypes.answer_text: When I'm in a bad mood or stressed, I literally cannot deal with ads — everything feels fake and annoying, especially those 'pamper yourself' body wash ads. I just scroll past aggressively. I'm only open to looking at new products when I'm already in a good headspace, like a lazy weekend when I'm browsing YouTube for fun. If I'm irritated, even my favorite brand's ad would annoy me. My picky side comes out hard when I'm not in the mood.
archetypes.why_plausible: She describes herself as 'picky' and 'sarcastic' (Q6), rates packaging claims at 1/5 trust (Q17), and is extremely brand-loyal with a 1/7 comfort trying new brands (Q7). This suggests a naturally skeptical disposition that could intensify under stress. Her going to a physical store rather than accepting a random substitute (Q26) shows she'd rather reject options than compromise when conditions aren't right. Her low trust in ads (TV ads: 2, online reviews: 2) supports defensive withdrawal.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin's concept scores would be highly variable depending on testing context. Concepts tested in a relaxed, unhurried setting would get fair, thoughtful evaluation. The same concept in a rushed or stressful context would be dismissed with low scores and sarcastic comments. She'd penalize concepts that feel 'try-hard' or overly aspirational, especially when not in the mood. Only concepts that lead with ingredients and dermatologist backing (her trusted sources) would survive a bad-mood filter.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Aspirational or emotional ads shown when she's stressed would backfire — she'd find them 'fake' and 'annoying,' rating them 2-3/10. The same ad on a relaxed weekend might get a 6-7/10. High-energy influencer ads would particularly irritate her in low moods. Only ingredient-breakdown or before/after content (Q57) would cut through regardless of mood, because it appeals to her rational, picky side rather than her emotional state.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: Moderate — mood creates momentary interest but loyalty and routine override impulse
archetypes.answer_text: Yeah, my mood definitely affects whether I notice an ad or not — if I'm relaxed and browsing Instagram on a weekend, I might actually watch a full body wash video and think 'hmm, nice.' But even then, I won't actually buy it because I already have my Lux and Baylis & Harding sorted. My moods make me window-shop mentally, but I always come back to what I know works for my sensitive skin. I might save a post when I'm in a good mood but I'll never impulse-buy from it.
archetypes.why_plausible: She is 'very loyal' to brands (Q16), always repurchases the same product (Q19), and rates her comfort trying new brands at 1/7 (Q7). Even when she tried D2C brands (Q48), she rated them all a mediocre 3. She acknowledges being moody but her actual purchase behavior is extremely stable — she'd only switch for a skin problem or doctor recommendation (Q22). Her friend recommendation trial likelihood is 1/7 (Q59), showing that even positive social influence doesn't override her routine.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: This twin would give moderate, consistent scores across mood states — slightly higher when relaxed, slightly lower when stressed, but never extreme. She'd intellectually appreciate a well-crafted concept (good ingredients, sensitive-skin claims, lavender notes) but wouldn't show high purchase intent unless it directly addressed a current skin problem. Concept scores would cluster in the 5-7 range regardless of context, making her a 'stable middle' respondent.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Ad engagement would vary by mood (she'd watch longer when relaxed, skip when busy), but conversion would remain consistently low regardless. A beautifully shot lavender body wash ad might get a 'saved' or 'liked' on a good day but wouldn't translate to purchase. Only ads that specifically trigger her switching criteria — dermatologist endorsement, sensitive-skin proof, or highlighting a problem with her current product — would move the needle, and mood would barely affect that rational evaluation.
